answer:
**Allama Iqbal's poem "Sach keh doon ae Brahman! gar tu bura na maane" (also known as "Naya Shivala" or "New Shivala") from *Bang-E-Dara Part 1* is a direct address to a Brahmin, critiquing idol worship and religious division while calling for unity through love of homeland and humanity.**[^1]

Here is the English translation:

> [p-752d60] O Brahmin, if you will not take offense,  
> Your ancient idols have lost reverence.  
> 
> [p-c536e2] From them you learned to hold your kin in scorn;  
> From God, the Preacher learned how war is born.  
> 
> [p-5af363] Fed up at last, I left both mosque and shrine,  
> The Preacher’s words, and those old tales of thine.  
> 
> [p-db56fb] You think your God is in a form of stone;  
> To me, my homeland's every speck is divine.  
> 
> [p-3a582d] Come, let us lift the veils of otherness anew,  
> And wipe away the lines that make us two.  
> 
> [p-425278] The city of the heart lies desolate,  
> Come, in this land a new shrine let's create.  
> 
> [p-948e14] Our pilgrimage must be the world's most high,  
> Its pinnacle should touch the very sky.  
> 
> [p-959af2] Let's rise each dawn to sing the sweetest prayer,  
> And serve the wine of love for all to share.  
> 
> [p-94fb2e] In faithful songs, both strength and peace reside;  
> For all this earth, salvation is love's guide.[^2]

Iqbal urges the Brahmin to abandon outdated divisions, proposing a new spiritual center in the homeland built on love (*preet*), which unites power (*shakti*) and peace (*shaanti*).

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 36 "Sach keh doon ae Brahman! gar tu bura na maane" (transliteration: "Sach keh doon ae Brahman! gar tu bura na maane").

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 36 (full passage: paragraphs p-752d60 through p-94fb2e).

args.query: Sach Keh Doon Ae Brahman Gar Tu Bura Na Maane
